sentences.text: Yes.
That-- that's right.
Because I was afraid if I'm going to jump and I'll be shot, and she may notice through somehow, or she'll hear machine guns.
She'll think, you know, I'm-- I don't want her to see if something happened to me.
So I went in in that car.
But the train started to roll.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The doors were locked in the cattle trains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went close to that little window with small little irons on the car, the cattle car, you know.
And we pulled it off.
It was not heavy wires.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my friend, the first one, Bianke, reach-- push through the head.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he jumped.
He was the first one.
He jumped.
And soon he jumped, we heard machine guns.
When we didn't hear no more like the regular train tch tch tch.
We heard heavy, like some five, six machine guns.
We didn't know on the roof of the train the SS with heavy duty machine guns.
They are all placed.
And another situation is the same like we planned to jump from the train, and where did we jump?
We knew where to jump.
We were jumping when we came to an area, it's called [LITHUANIAN].
We-- we knew in these forests are the Soviet partisans.
And soon, we jump from the train near this area.
We are going in in the woods.
And we are going be saved with our lives.
Where the Soviets army.
And there was already a lot of Jewish people, the last people from the ghetto, who were in this wood walking with the partisans together, shooting, you know, the German army in the back.
But when I heard the machine guns, and then other cattle trains, some other people had the same idea too.
When they came to the forest.
Not only we had idea.
Other young people had the same idea too.
So when it was a lot of shooting, the train stopped.
The Germans stopped the train.
They opened up the doors from the cattle train.
They walked in.
They pushed themselves in, because it's so loaded that it was hard for two, three people to go in there.
We all had to stand.
There was no place to sit down.
And they took out from each cattle train like three, four people.
They laid them down in the side of the railroad, and they killed.
And they said, everybody to the trains.
And they opened up the gate.
They were driving off to Germany in the train-- in the cattle trains.
My mother stood a little by the door too.
And further up she say another boy dragging in who was shot from far away.
She didn't-- and it looked like me a little, too, like a blond skinny guy like me.
She start hysterically crying.
My brother and sister couldn't stop helping crying.
So she says, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Morris is dead.
I saw him.
They were dragging him.
They were dragging him in in the train.
He's there.
I know Maishke's head.
I know that's him.
Children said, no, that was not him.
That was another guy who looks-- boy who looks like Maishke.
And to that day, I feel very sorry.
Because my mother died [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in her mind that I was shot at that transport.
And I was in the other cattle trains there.
My sister and my brother-- and when my sister met my mother in Auschwitz before my mother was cremated, my mother said, war, she says, is my life.
Maishke is dead anyhow.
I saw him, the way they dragged him.
I don't care no more for my life, what happening.
Because she was already in-- in Stutthof a couple of weeks.
No food, nothing.
They took away her shoes.
And with my little brother, she was-- took away the whole spirit.
He was no more human.
They made you no more to be human being.
So my mother told Faiga, my sister, and Miriam, my other sister, yeah, she says.
Maishke is dead.
I am dead.
And that bothers me a lot of times.
And the cattle train was closed again.
All the doors back.
And it kept on rolling and rolling and rolling, and all that time it rolled we were all figuring we are seeing death in front of us.
In the daytime when we passed by and I looked through the little holes, and I saw big factories with big chimneys, I said, here.
I think that's the place.
I think this, we are going to be cremated.
Because we knew already the last years that it was crematoriums.
We knew in the ghetto.
The first years of the war, we didn't know about crematoriums.
By the last half a year in the ghetto, before liquidations, we hears rumors.
You know, rumors travels.
Where one escapes.
Where one German is drunk and he talks, you know.
Rumor started.
We knew the last half a year in the ghetto that they had crematoriums in Poland and other places, and people are cremated by the thousands, and by the hundreds of thousands.
So any time-- excuse me-- when I passed and I looked through the little cracks, while the train was rolling and I see a factory.
I said, oh.
I think the train is going to stop here.
That must be the crematorium, with big chimneys, you know.
But it was factories, you know.
And I looked at in other places, the fields.
You see, it was in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in July. 14, you know.
July 13 and July 14.
The weather was nice.
We start to go in further already to Germany.
The farmers and the fields and the cows and the beautiful nature.
And I looked through the window and I says, what a beautiful world.
And I'm-- we are all going to be killed.
And tears came to my eyes.
I said, beautiful world.
Yeah.
I said, look.
Why does these people live?
Looking at the farms and the fields and the animals and the cows, and the hay, and the-- and the plants.
Why are we different?
Why are going-- why are they taking us to kill us?
Did the train stop at all?
It stopped at certain stations, you know, like railroad changing or something like that.
Because it stopped in one place, and I had a little metal can.
It was maybe beef before in that can.
It was laying in the train somewheres.
And I pushed through a little can, and a woman, a German woman was standing there.
And we were for a while there, maybe 20 minutes, or 15 or 25 minutes.
The train was-- I don't know, maybe getting something.
Food for the Germans or something.
And I push through the-- through the hole that little can, to give me water.
Because a lot of dead people are already in the train from thirst.
And my mother, when we left the ghetto, gave us some money.
And my mother cut in my leather boots here, we-- I was wearing leather boots like that.
Tall boots.
She cut a little opening, and she pushed in rubles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think a couple of Swiss francs.
And a couple of dollars, American money my mother had.
We were poor people, but my mother was secretly saving all the time, you know, on the side.
I think I got her nature.
You understand.
And she said, Kinder, Kinder, children.
You never know when this can help you sometimes.
She gave it to me.
She gave it to my brother.
And she gave it to my sisters.
And I pulled out a-- a paper bill.
I don't know even what it was.
If it was a ruble or a Swiss franc or a dollar, an American dollar.
I know it was a paper money.
And I pushed through that little crack.
And I say to the lady, give me a little water in the can.
And I wasn't surprised.
She brought me water.
In a rusted can.
While I was drinking the water, people were jumped at me.
They-- they almost killed me.
To get just one lick of the water.
And from the water, I almost had maybe-- not even an eighth of a can.
I was licking the can of the water till I actually came to Dachau.
I was licking the can with my tongue.
It was no more water.
It was actually dried out, that can, and I was still licking.
I thought maybe from a crick.
It's rusted and bent.
Maybe a drop of water will come out.
I think maybe I was hallucinating.
I think so.
When I start to think now that the can was so dried out, and I was still going in with my mouth and my tongue.
I think it was hallucinating that the can still-- it-- it make water, or it has water.
And I kept that little can in my hand till I arrived in Dachau.
When I arrived in at Dachau, then I met my brother.
My mother was separated on the train in Stutthof.
Maybe a day before we came to Dachau.
They took out a lot of-- stop the train in Stutthof.
And they took out all the women and children from the trains.
And they took them to that-- to the crematoriums of Stutthof.
Yeah.
My sisters too.
Yeah.
My little sister was too young.
That's what I learned after the war.
She was 13 years old, and when they took my mother with my brother and my little sister to the crematorium, they marched her on.
A transport of women was going to work.
My mother took my little sister, Miriam, and she pushed her in in the group of the women who were going-- marching on to work.
And that's how my little sister remained alive.
Otherwise, she would have gone in with my mother and little brother in the crematorium.
They were in Auschwitz.
And then they took all the women to work, and mine-- and then they took them in different camps.
And that's how my sister remained-- the young one-- alive.
Because my mother pushed her out from the line as they were taken to the crematorium.
And they were marching on a transport of women going to work.
Not to the crematorium.
So my mother gave her a push.
And she says, in.
And she pushed her in, and my little sister got all mangled up between the women who go to work.
And the other women embraced her.
And that's how my little sister, who is in Israel, remained alive.
And that train rolled down after they dropped off all the women in Stutthof.
Some of the women went in Stutthof and they remained alive because they were transport to working camps.

But some of the women who had children who didn't want to give up the children-- if my mother would have give away my little brother, she maybe would have remained and stayed alive too, because women without children, they took a lot of them to different camps for work, labor, slave labor.
But my mother's-- the German came here.
He says, gibt mir dein Kind.
My mother said, nein.
This is my baby.
I don't give him away.
I go where my baby goes.
[GERMAN].
Pushed her on right with all the kids and with all the women and with the old people, and with all the mothers with children.
And she went right into the crematorium.
Didn't want to give up my brother, Solomon.
My sister Miriam told--
Yeah.
My sister, yes.
She was still-- you know, hearing the stories from the other people.
There was a lot of women in the camp who were kapos.
That's how-- they told Miriam.
They told my sister.
My sister still remained in Stutthof like four, five days till they took them away in different camps.
So my sister came back from work with the people.
Where is my mother?
So some of the-- some of the police, you know, women there, who worked, you know, with the Germans-- I mean, they were there to do the job, you know.
Some of the prisoners said, my Kind, my child, your mother marched on.
Yeah.
We arrived in Dachau.
And that was the 15th of July, 1941.
And we were unloaded from the trains.
And the commandant was by the railroad already with a group of SS.
And with the heavy guard, taken right by the train.
And we marched on right to Dachau.
And when we came to the gates on Dachau, the gates opened up.
And we were chased-- again I'm using the word chased, because we had to run all the time.
Because you didn't know where are going to get hurt.
[SPEAKING GERMAN], schnell, schnell.
All the time, schnell, you know.
To make a big, you know, rush on you.
You should not be able to think of anything.
And when the-- went in on Dachau, and the gates closed, we no more human beings.
We were numbers and we were dead people.
First, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they start to take away-- they start cutting the hair.
Take off your hair right away.
Yeah.
Excuse me.
The first day.
Start to take away, cutting the hair.
First.
Then they start to take away your clothes.
Then they start to tell you to give away all valuables, and no play, no tricks.
And don't swallow valuables.
We have a way to find.
They put the-- threw a very big scare on you.
And they shot already a couple of people right away.
The last 10 minutes-- the first 10 minutes.
To scare you.
Right away they shot people for nothing, you know.
In the-- in the Platz there, the camp.
And took away the clothes.
Then we had to go.
They gave you striped clothes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then you went through, you know, to little tables there.
And they gave you numbers on your clothes, on your clothes.
My number was 81840.
My brother's number was 81841.
And so on and so on.
And we got the clothes, the concentration camp clothes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me.
Then they took us to a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to show us where the temporary barracks are.
They didn't have enough barracks.
So they made temporary round little, you know, like from masonite to the roof, temporary barracks.
You belong eight-- like the numbers. 81840 to 880.
Like 855 belongs to number four.
That belong to number six.
So we went to the little barracks.
Was no floor.
It was earth.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we wearing the uniforms.
We didn't have anything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me.
And then it started, you know, all the murder situations, you know.
Beatings, hunger, no food.
We didn't have-- in one week, your spirit was broken.
You were a nobody.
And after 10 days, you start to see people dying in the yard.
Germans, the commandant was a murderer, a killer.
He was not a young man.
I was surprised, you know.
He was a man maybe 55 years old.
He should have known the world.
He must've learned what life is all about, and human beings.
He didn't know about those things.
Johaan Kirsh.
Johaan Kirsh.
They gave him a nickname.
All the prisoners gave him a nickname.
I'm a very bashful man to say that to you.
But it's religious, why I got to tell you, why I must say that name.
He didn't call us Juden or other things.
From the first day we marched in, he called us [GERMAN].
You listen.
Every word I say, you [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] is a bad word.
He knows what it is.
[GERMAN].
In the United States, when I hear that word, I get very angry, when people say.
Asshole.
I cannot stand that word.
When I hear somebody-- I hear sometime on television, that word.
My face gets red.
He knocked the people down on the ground, and when they laid on the ground, he kicked so much in the head that a man was dead.
And that was his way of killing prisoners.
He killed my friend, Moishe Baliban.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I left it.
He killed my friend Moishe Baliban because when we came from work one day, where we were working outside the camp.
We-- they took us to work in the Messerschmitt factory [GERMAN].
And we came back from work before we go in through the gate, we had to take off the hats.
And the Germans used to take guards by the gate, and used to say, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Them That means, hats off.
We had to fast take off the hats.
And then [SPEAKING GERMAN].
You put back on the hat.
So my friend didn't take off his hat, Moishe Baliban.
So fast the hat.
And he noticed that.
Because that Moishe Baliban was already sick.
He was already-- was an older man.
Was not a young fellow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse me.
He was-- he didn't take off the hat fast.
And he took him out from the row, you know.
Dropped him on the floor.
And he kicked him so hard, killed him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Moishe Baliban died by Johaan Kirsh with his boots.
He killed him.
And that was the killing in that camp.
And then the hunger was so big, that people start to-- you were a nothing, you know.
Dying like flies.
If you get a piece of bread for a whole day with a little water, you get skinnier and skinnier.
You-- you die.
Everybody start to become skeletons.
No.
Not in the beginning.
The beginning, I worked in that place mall.
Where they were building-- they claimed they are going to build there the secret weapon which is going to destroy the United States and the whole world, wherever they want to be to be destroyed.
It's going to be built in that factory.
That's what they were building.
It was working 24 hours steady.
It's called [GERMAN].
And it was managed by the Todd Company.
I think it's T-O-D-D.
Todd Company.
They managed that manufacturing plant [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for the secret weapons they said we're going to make there.
And it was going on day and night.
Millions-- hundreds of thousands of people died, you know, in these constructions.
I was working by the cement.
After the-- they were pumping from a station, day and night cement, for the roof.
So American airplanes cannot bomb it through.
So we are building roofs, rounded roofs, which it was the height approximately about 10 foot solid concrete roof, rounded.
Underneath was taken out with machines all the earth, maybe 10 foot deep.
Taken out, day and night, earth, for miles.
And then when it was taken out, all the earth, then was made with forms roofs about 10 foot thick of cement.
So it had to pump day and night, that cement.
And I was with the piece of wood with a long handle, had to stomp the cement, it shouldn't fall in one place, to spread it.
To spread it.
So let's stop now.
And that was the work.
